"Notts County F.C."), [Stoke City](/wiki/Stoke_City_F.C. "Stoke City F.C."), [Barnsley](/wiki/Barnsley_F.C. "Barnsley F.C.") and [Crewe Alexandra](/wiki/Crewe_Alexandra_F.C. **Guðjón Þórðarson** (Icelandic pronunciation: [[ˈkvʏðjoun ˈθourðar̥sɔn]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Icelandic "Help:IPA/Icelandic"); born 14 September 1955), known in the United Kingdom as **Gudjon Thordarson**, is an Icelandic [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") manager and former player. He has previously been manager of [Iceland](/wiki/Iceland_national_football_team "Iceland national football team"), Icelandic clubs [ÍA](/wiki/%C3%8D%C3%BEr%C3%B3ttabandalag_Akraness "Íþróttabandalag Akraness"), [KA](/wiki/Knattspyrnuf%C3%A9lag_Akureyrar "Knattspyrnufélag Akureyrar"), [KR](/wiki/Knattspyrnuf%C3%A9lag_Reykjav%C3%ADkur "Knattspyrnufélag Reykjavíkur"), [Keflavík](/wiki/Keflav%C3%ADk_Football_Club "Keflavík Football Club"), [BÍ/Bolungarvík](/wiki/B%C3%8D/Bolungarv%C3%ADk "BÍ/Bolungarvík") and [Grindavík](/wiki/Grindav%C3%ADk_men%27s_football "Grindavík men's football"), English clubs [Notts County](/wiki/Notts_County_F.C. "Notts County F.C."), [Stoke City](/wiki/Stoke_City_F.C. "Stoke City F.C."), [Barnsley](/wiki/Barnsley_F.C. "Barnsley F.C.") and [Crewe Alexandra](/wiki/Crewe_Alexandra_F.C. "Crewe Alexandra F.C."), Norwegian club [Start](/wiki/I.K._Start "I.K. Start") and Faroese club [NSÍ Runavík](/wiki/NS%C3%8D_Runav%C3%ADk "NSÍ Runavík").[[1]](#cite_note-1)

Guðjón has three sons who have also played professional football, namely: [Bjarni](/wiki/Bjarni_Gu%C3%B0j%C3%B3nsson "Bjarni Guðjónsson"), [Joey](/wiki/J%C3%B3hannes_Karl_Gu%C3%B0j%C3%B3nsson "Jóhannes Karl Guðjónsson") and [Þórður Guðjónsson](/wiki/%C3%9E%C3%B3r%C3%B0ur_Gu%C3%B0j%C3%B3nsson "Þórður Guðjónsson"). His grandson [Ísak Bergmann Jóhannesson](/wiki/%C3%8Dsak_Bergmann_J%C3%B3hannesson "Ísak Bergmann Jóhannesson") is a current professional player.

## Playing career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gu%C3%B0j%C3%B3n_%C3%9E%C3%B3r%C3%B0arson&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Playing career")]

Guðjón played 212 league matches for his hometown club, [ÍA Akranes](/wiki/%C3%8DA_Akranes "ÍA Akranes").[[2]](#cite_note-2) In his career, Gudjon won five league titles and five cup titles. He also played 22 European games for ÍA Akranes.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Guðjón played in his only international match in 1985.

## Managerial career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gu%C3%B0j%C3%B3n_%C3%9E%C3%B3r%C3%B0arson&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Managerial career")]

### In Iceland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gu%C3%B0j%C3%B3n_%C3%9E%C3%B3r%C3%B0arson&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: In Iceland")]

Guðjón's last season as a player was 1986 and his first coaching job was at [ÍA Akranes](/wiki/%C3%8DA_Akranes "ÍA Akranes") in the 1987 season. Guðjón then left off to [Akureyri](/wiki/Akureyri "Akureyri") to become manager of [KA Akureyri](/wiki/Knattspyrnuf%C3%A9lag_Akureyrar "Knattspyrnufélag Akureyrar") in 1988. The team won its first and only [league](/wiki/%C3%9Arvalsdeild "Úrvalsdeild") title surprisingly in 1989. That has to be known as a big achievement for Guðjón given the players he had in his squad. After being relegated in 1990, ÍA Akranes appointed Guðjón as manager. The team was promoted at first attempt, and subsequently won the [Icelandic league](/wiki/%C3%9Arvalsdeild "Úrvalsdeild") the following year, in 1992. The team dominated Icelandic football the next few years and became [league champions](/wiki/%C3%9Arvalsdeild "Úrvalsdeild") of Iceland for five years running, 1992–1996.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

[KR Reykjavik](/wiki/Knattspyrnuf%C3%A9lag_Reykjav%C3%ADkur "Knattspyrnufélag Reykjavíkur"), Iceland's oldest and most successful club, appointed Guðjón after the 1993 season. The team had not won the [Icelandic league](/wiki/%C3%9Arvalsdeild_karla_(football) "Úrvalsdeild karla (football)") for more than 20 years and Guðjón was seen as the right man to win the title. He did not win the league in his two-year stint at the club, but the team did however win the [Icelandic Cup](/wiki/Icelandic_Men%27s_Football_Cup "Icelandic Men's Football Cup") on both occasions 1994 and 1995. Guðjón came back to Akranes after his spell at KR and guided the team to win the Icelandic league and the Cup in 1996. After the season, actually late in November 1996 ÍA Akranes terminated his contract. But Guðjón wasn't out of a job for too long. In June 1997 he took charge of the [Iceland national team](/wiki/Iceland_national_football_team "Iceland national football team"). Guðjón was successful as manager of the Iceland national team. During the three years he was in charge, the team played 24 games, winning ten of them, drawing four and losing eight. The team scored 35 goals in the process, conceding 23.

The team was close to qualifying to the [European Championship in 2000](/wiki/Euro_2000 "Euro 2000"), despite being placed in a strong qualifying group alongside [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine_national_football_team "Ukraine national football team"), [Russia](/wiki/Russia_national_football_team "Russia national football team") and current world champions [France](/wiki/France_national_football_team "France national football team"). The game that took place at Stade de France was one that put Icelandic football to a higher standard. After being 2–0 down at half time, to come back too 2–2, and closely losing in additional time 3–2.

### Stoke City

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gu%C3%B0j%C3%B3n_%C3%9E%C3%B3r%C3%B0arson&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Stoke City")]

Guðjón became [Stoke City](/wiki/Stoke_City_F.C. "Stoke City F.C.") manager in November 1999, when he joined the club following the successful acquisition of the club by a group of Icelandic businessmen headed by [Gunnar Gíslason](/wiki/Gunnar_G%C3%ADslason_(businessman) "Gunnar Gíslason (businessman)").[[5]](#cite_note-The_Sentinel_review-5) They decided to bring in their own man to replace the existing manager [Gary Megson](/wiki/Gary_Megson "Gary Megson"). Guðjón brought in a number of his fellow countrymen to the club with the arrival of [Einar Daníelsson](/wiki/Einar_Dan%C3%ADelsson "Einar Daníelsson"), [Sigursteinn Gíslason](/wiki/Sigursteinn_G%C3%ADslason "Sigursteinn Gíslason"), [Brynjar Gunnarsson](/wiki/Brynjar_Gunnarsson "Brynjar Gunnarsson") and his son [Bjarni Guðjónsson](/wiki/Bjarni_Gu%C3%B0j%C3%B3nsson "Bjarni Guðjónsson"). He got off to a perfect start as Stoke manager as his side beat [Wycombe Wanderers](/wiki/Wycombe_Wanderers_F.C. "Wycombe Wanderers F.C.") 4–0 on 23 November 1999. Good results continued as more Scandinavian players arrived at the club such as [Frode Kippe](/wiki/Frode_Kippe "Frode Kippe"), [Mikael Hansson](/wiki/Mikael_Hansson "Mikael Hansson") and [Arnar Gunnlaugsson](/wiki/Arnar_Gunnlaugsson "Arnar Gunnlaugsson").[[5]](#cite_note-The_Sentinel_review-5) Stoke also reached the [2000 Football League Trophy Final](/wiki/2000_Football_League_Trophy_Final "2000 Football League Trophy Final") where they faced [Bristol City](/wiki/Bristol_City_F.C. "Bristol City F.C.") in front of 75,057 at [Wembley](/wiki/Wembley_Stadium_(1923) "Wembley Stadium (1923)"). Goals from [Graham Kavanagh](/wiki/Graham_Kavanagh "Graham Kavanagh") and [Peter Thorne](/wiki/Peter_Thorne_(English_footballer) "Peter Thorne (English footballer)") earned Stoke a 2–1 victory. In the league Stoke finished in sixth position reaching the play-offs where they faced [Gillingham](/wiki/Gillingham_F.C. "Gillingham F.C."). Stoke won the first leg 3–2 but two controversial refereeing decisions cost Stoke dearly and they lost the second leg 3–0.[[5]](#cite_note-The_Sentinel_review-5)

In [2000–01](/wiki/2000%E2%80%9301_Stoke_City_F.C._season "2000–01 Stoke City F.C. season") Guðjón was again busy bringing in Icelandic players with [Ríkharður Daðason](/wiki/R%C3%ADkhar%C3%B0ur_Da%C3%B0ason "Ríkharður Daðason"), [Stefán Þórðarson](/wiki/Stef%C3%A1n_%C3%9E%C3%B3r%C3%B0arson "Stefán Þórðarson"), [Birkir Kristinsson](/wiki/Birkir_Kristinsson "Birkir Kristinsson") and Danish defender [Henrik Risom](/wiki/Henrik_Risom "Henrik Risom") all agreeing to move to [Stoke-on-Trent](/wiki/Stoke-on-Trent "Stoke-on-Trent"). Results were often inconsistent meaning automatic promotion was not possible and Stoke made it into the play-offs.[[5]](#cite_note-The_Sentinel_review-5) They faced [Walsall](/wiki/Walsall_F.C. "Walsall F.C.") who, after a 0–0 draw in the first leg, beat Stoke 4–2, meaning that Stoke faced a fourth season in the third tier. The pressure was on Guðjón to gain automatic promotion in [2001–02](/wiki/2001%E2%80%9302_Stoke_City_F.C._season "2001–02 Stoke City F.C. season") and he tried a different approach. He sold both [Graham Kavanagh](/wiki/Graham_Kavanagh "Graham Kavanagh") and Peter Thorne to [Cardiff City](/wiki/Cardiff_City_F.C. "Cardiff City F.C.") for a combined fee of £2.7 million and with that money he brought in defender [Peter Handyside](/wiki/Peter_Handyside "Peter Handyside") from [Grimsby Town](/wiki/Grimsby_Town_F.C. "Grimsby Town F.C.") who was made captain, goalkeeper [Neil Cutler](/wiki/Neil_Cutler "Neil Cutler"), Belarusian defender [Sergei Shtanyuk](/wiki/Syarhey_Shtanyuk "Syarhey Shtanyuk"), Belgian midfielder [Jurgen Vandeurzen](/wiki/Jurgen_Vandeurzen "Jurgen Vandeurzen"), [David Rowson](/wiki/David_Rowson "David Rowson") from [Aberdeen](/wiki/Aberdeen_F.C. "Aberdeen F.C.") and most impressively former Dutch international [Peter Hoekstra](/wiki/Peter_Hoekstra_(footballer) "Peter Hoekstra (footballer)").[[5]](#cite_note-The_Sentinel_review-5) Stoke were again lacking the consistency for automatic promotion and for the third season running they entered the play-offs. The opponents this time were Cardiff and it looked like being another failure as the Bluebirds won the 1st leg at Stoke 2–1.[[5]](#cite_note-The_Sentinel_review-5) Stoke went into the second leg at the notoriously hostile [Ninian Park](/wiki/Ninian_Park "Ninian Park") 2–1 down and with both sides missing chances the match was 0–0 after 90 minutes and in the final minute of injury time [James O'Connor](/wiki/James_O%27Connor_(footballer,_born_1979) "James O'Connor (footballer, born 1979)") scored to send the match into extra time.[[5]](#cite_note-The_Sentinel_review-5) And Stoke scored again via an O'Connor shot which deflected in off [Souleymane Oularé](/wiki/Souleymane_Oular%C3%A9 "Souleymane Oularé") to give Stoke the most dramatic victory.[[5]](#cite_note-The_Sentinel_review-5) The [final](/wiki/2002_Football_League_Second_Division_play-off_Final "2002 Football League Second Division play-off Final") against [Brentford](/wiki/Brentford_F.C. "Brentford F.C.") was not as dramatic, as Stoke won comfortably 2–0 thanks to goals from [Deon Burton](/wiki/Deon_Burton "Deon Burton") and an own goal from [Ben Burgess](/wiki/Ben_Burgess "Ben Burgess").[[5]](#cite_note-The_Sentinel_review-5) However, despite finally achieving promotion via the play-offs at the third attempt Guðjón's contract was not renewed.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

### Start

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gu%C3%B0j%C3%B3n_%C3%9E%C3%B3r%C3%B0arson&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Start")]

Guðjón signed a contract with Start[[7]](#cite_note-7) in Kristiansand just through the end of the season to try to steer the club away from relegation. However relegation was inevitable. But at the time Guðjón spent there five of the young players in the squad were called up for the U21 Norway side for the first time.

### Barnsley

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gu%C3%B0j%C3%B3n_%C3%9E%C3%B3r%C3%B0arson&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Barnsley")]

[Barnsley](/wiki/Barnsley_F.C. "Barnsley F.C.") appointed Guðjón as manager in 2003. The club had a great start to the season, but his reign ended after a Peter Ridsdale takeover at the club. Guðjón was fired in 2004.

### Keflavík

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gu%C3%B0j%C3%B3n_%C3%9E%C3%B3r%C3%B0arson&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Keflavík")]

Guðjón decided to return to Iceland in 2005 and was appointed manager of [Keflavik](/wiki/Keflav%C3%ADk_Football_Club "Keflavík Football Club"). However, he quit just three days before the first game of the season to take charge of [Notts County](/wiki/Notts_County_F.C. "Notts County F.C.").

### Notts County

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gu%C3%B0j%C3%B3n_%C3%9E%C3%B3r%C3%B0arson&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Notts County")]

Guðjón was appointed manager of [Notts County](/wiki/Notts_County_F.C. "Notts County F.C.") in 2005. The team started well, leading the table early on, but eventually failed to make the playoffs. Guðjón resigned his post at Notts County in May 2006 after Notts County finished in 21st place in League Two and in 89th place overall, the lowest position the club had ever finished. Guðjón became the club's first non-British manager when he arrived at Meadow Lane in May 2005.

### ÍA Akranes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gu%C3%B0j%C3%B3n_%C3%9E%C3%B3r%C3%B0arson&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: ÍA Akranes")]

[ÍA Akranes](/wiki/%C3%8DA_Akranes "ÍA Akranes") had a difficult season in 2006, avoiding relegation in the last few weeks of the season. Guðjón was appointed manager ahead of the 2007 season, where the team finished third in the league, with one of the league's youngest teams and also scoring the third most goals in the league. In June 2008, the media in Scotland repeatedly linked Guðjón with the manager's post of [Heart of Midlothian](/wiki/Heart_of_Midlothian_F.C. "Heart of Midlothian F.C."),[[8]](#cite_note-8) with [Terry Butcher](/wiki/Terry_Butcher "Terry Butcher") as his assistant, but Akranes officials insisted it was only rumours and he would stay put in Iceland. Guðjón himself did, however, confirm to an Icelandic news-site that discussions with Hearts had indeed taken place[[9]](#cite_note-9) and that he was considering his options. On 11 July, Hearts appointed [Csaba Laszlo](/wiki/Csaba_L%C3%A1szl%C3%B3_(footballer,_born_1964) "Csaba László (footballer, born 1964)") as the new manager.[[10]](#cite_note-10) Whether Guðjón had ever been a serious contender for the job is not known.

On 21 July, Guðjón was fired from his post at ÍA.[[11]](#cite_note-11) The team's performance had been going straight downhill for weeks, but the final straw was a 6–1 defeat against [Breiðablik](/wiki/Breidablik_UBK "Breidablik UBK"). Guðjón was replaced by the brothers, [Arnar Gunnlaugsson](/wiki/Arnar_Gunnlaugsson "Arnar Gunnlaugsson") and [Bjarki Gunnlaugsson](/wiki/Bjarki_Gunnlaugsson "Bjarki Gunnlaugsson").[[12]](#cite_note-12)

### Crewe Alexandra

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gu%C3%B0j%C3%B3n_%C3%9E%C3%B3r%C3%B0arson&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Crewe Alexandra")]

Guðjón was announced as new manager of [League One](/wiki/Football_League_One "Football League One") side [Crewe Alexandra](/wiki/Crewe_Alexandra_F.C. "Crewe Alexandra F.C.") on 24 December 2008, as a permanent replacement for the recently sacked [Steve Holland](/wiki/Steve_Holland_(footballer) "Steve Holland (footballer)"). However, previous caretaker manager [Dario Gradi](/wiki/Dario_Gradi "Dario Gradi") remained in charge of the team for two games during the Christmas period, with Guðjón taking over on 29 December.[[13]](#cite_note-Thordarson_appointed_Crewe_boss-13)

Despite improvements on the pitch, which saw him win manager of the month for February[[14]](#cite_note-14) he could not prevent the club from being relegated into [League Two](/wiki/Football_League_Two "Football League Two"). The poor form continued in the new season, and Guðjón was sacked as Crewe manager on 2 October 2009.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

### Later career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gu%C3%B0j%C3%B3n_%C3%9E%C3%B3r%C3%B0arson&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Later career")]

In July 2020, he took over as the manager of [1. deild karla](/wiki/1._deild_karla_(football) "1. deild karla (football)") club [Víkingur Ólafsvík](/wiki/Ungmennaf%C3%A9lagi%C3%B0_V%C3%ADkingur "Ungmennafélagið Víkingur") after the firing of Jón Páll Pálmason.[[16]](#cite_note-16) Despite initial interest by both parties for him to continue with the team following the season, the team eventually hired Gunnar Einarsson. In July 2021, Gunnar was fired with the team in last place after the first nine games and Guðjón was hired again.[[17]](#cite_note-17) His contract was not renewed after the team finished 7th in the [2. deild karla](/wiki/2._deild_karla_(football) "2. deild karla (football)") in 2022.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

## Controversies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gu%C3%B0j%C3%B3n_%C3%9E%C3%B3r%C3%B0arson&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Controversies")]

Guðjón Þórðarson has been involved in many controversies in his career as a manager. He has often been involved in board difficulties.[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22)

On 13 May 2005, he resigned as manager of [Keflavík](/wiki/Keflav%C3%ADk_Football_Club "Keflavík Football Club") only a few days before the [2005 season](/wiki/%C3%9Arvalsdeild_2005 "Úrvalsdeild 2005") began, because of unfulfilled financial and professional obligations.[[23]](#cite_note-23) Keflavík disputed those accusations.[[24]](#cite_note-24) Guðjón had repeatedly denied being in talks with [English League 2](/wiki/Football_League_Two "Football League Two") club [Notts County](/wiki/Notts_County_F.C. "Notts County F.C.") at the time.[[25]](#cite_note-25) However, one day after leaving his job, on 14 May 2005, he admitted being interested in the Notts County job,[[26]](#cite_note-26) and was subsequently appointed manager of Notts County on 17 May 2005.[[27]](#cite_note-27)
